Let me approach this systematically by writing a Python script to process all the data. The key insight is:\n\n1. Each maintenance work order has a technician who works on a specific machine at a specific time\n2. After maintenance, the machine's condition can change (introduce a hidden fault)\n3. A hidden fault affects batches produced on that machine until the next maintenance\n4. Quality rejections happen - some are due to these hidden faults\n5. We need to link rejected batches to the maintenance event that caused the fault\n6. A technician is confirmed if ≥3 distinct rejected batches across ≥2 different work orders can be linked to their maintenance\n7. Then report products for each confirmed technician\n\nThe approach is complex because:\n- Hidden faults are never labeled\n- We need to infer which maintenance events caused quality issues\n- The same fault class may affect different products differently\n- We need to rule out non-maintenance factors\n\nLet me write a comprehensive Python script that:\n1. Reads all data\n2. Builds a timeline of maintenance events per machine\n3. Links batches to the maintenance period they were produced in\n4. Identifies rejected batches (comparing to quality threshold)\n5. For each rejected batch, identifies candidate maintenance events\n6. Confirms technicians meeting the thresholds\n7. Maps confirmed technicians to affected products\n\nThe challenge is determining which rejected batches are caused by maintenance (hidden faults) vs. other factors. The problem states:\n- We need to rule out defects from unrelated factors and natural variation\n- A given fault class may affect only some products\n\nLet me think about this more carefully. The key statement is:\n\"Linking a rejected batch to a maintenance event: A batch can be attributed to one specific maintenance event only when all three hold: a) the batch was produced on the machine that the technician serviced, b) production started after that maintenance was completed, c) production started before the next maintenance event on the same machine.\"\n\nSo essentially, for a given batch, we look at the machine it was produced on, and the maintenance event that most recently completed on that machine before the batch started. That batch falls in the \"maintenance window\" of that specific event.\n\n\"Confirming a technician: A technician is confirmed only if their own maintenance work can be linked to at least 3 distinct rejected batches, distributed across at least 2 different work orders performed by that same technician.\"\n\nSo we need to find maintenance events where:\n- The technician performed the work\n- There are ≥3 rejected batches that fall in the window after that maintenance (and before the next maintenance on the same machine)\n- Those ≥3 batches are distributed across ≥2 different work orders\n\nWait, I think the interpretation needs to be: For a technician, we look at ALL their maintenance work orders. For each work order, we look at batches produced on the same machine after the maintenance completed and before the next maintenance on that machine. Among those batches, some are rejected. If across all their work, a technician has ≥3 distinct rejected batches linked to their work, AND these batches come from ≥2 different work orders (i.e., not all from a single maintenance job), then the technician is confirmed.\n\n\"Once a technician is confirmed, include every product for which at least one rejected batch can be causally linked to that technician's faulty maintenance\"\n\nSo for each confirmed technician, we list all products that had at least one rejected batch linked to that technician's maintenance.\n\nThe tricky part is the \"hidden fault\" concept and \"A given fault class may affect only some products.\" This means not every rejected batch in a maintenance window is necessarily caused by the maintenance - the fault class introduced by the maintenance might only affect certain products.\n\nLet me look at the data more carefully. The quality_inspections.csv has defect codes (D_001 through D_014). Each maintenance event might introduce a particular kind of defect. Different products might only be affected by specific defect types.\n\nActually wait - the problem says \"Hidden faults are never labeled as such in the data.\" And \"A given fault class may affect only some products.\" So the data doesn't tell us which maintenance introduced which fault. We need to infer this.\n\nOne approach: For each maintenance event, look at the defect codes seen in rejected batches after that maintenance. If the same defect code pattern emerges repeatedly after a specific technician's work, that's evidence.\n\nBut actually, let me think about this differently. The problem is essentially asking us to identify which technicians are responsible for quality issues. The simplest possible interpretation that works:\n\n1. For each batch, determine which maintenance event it falls after\n2. If the batch is rejected, flag it\n3. For each technician, count rejected batches in their maintenance windows\n4. If ≥3 rejected batches across ≥2 work orders, confirm them\n5. Report the products\n\nBut we also need to handle the product-family / fault-class specificity. The defect code in the inspection result tells us the type of defect. If a maintenance introduces a hidden fault of a certain class (defect code), it would only cause rejections for products that are susceptible to that defect.\n\nHmm, but actually the problem says \"A given fault class may affect only some products.\" This is about the fault class that the maintenance introduces. The same fault might affect product A but not product B on the same machine. So when counting rejected batches for confirming a technician, we should count only those where the product is susceptible to the fault that was introduced.\n\nBut we don't know which faults were introduced! We need to infer from the data.\n\nWait, let me re-read more carefully. The key relationships:\n\n1. Maintenance event on a machine → may introduce hidden fault (certain defect code)\n2. That defect code → affects only some products (based on product family/product properties)\n3. Affected products → will show rejections with that defect code\n4. {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1781041090299,"sessionID":"ses_151af1bb2ffeU77q8xSk3g0087","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"write","callID":"call_00_TUMX1i2wG9AU5CBa0rQa3328","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"content":"import csv\nimport json\nfrom collections import defaultdict, Counter\n\n# ── 1. Read all data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\ndef read_csv(path):\n    with open(path) as f:\n        return list(csv.DictReader(f))\n\nmachines      = read_csv('/app/machines.csv')\nproducts      = read_csv('/app/products.csv')\ntechnicians   = read_csv('/app/technicians.csv')\nwork_orders   = read_csv('/app/maintenance_work_orders.csv')\nassignments   = read_csv('/app/machine_assignment_log.csv')\nbatches       = read_csv('/app/production_batches.csv')\ninspections   = read_csv('/app/quality_inspections.csv')\nfactory_cond  = read_csv('/app/daily_factory_conditions.csv')\n\nproduct_info = {r['product_id']: r for r in products}\nmachine_info = {r['machine_id']: r for r in machines}\n\n# ── 2. Parse service reports ──\n\ndef time_to_minutes(t):\n    parts = t.strip().split(':')\n    return int(parts[0]) * 60 + int(parts[1])\n\nreports = {}\nfor wo in work_orders:\n    rpath = wo['report_path']\n    try:\n        with open('/app/' + rpath) as f:\n            lines = f.readlines()\n        report = {}\n        for line in lines:\n            if ':' in line:\n                k, v = line.strip().split(':', 1)\n                report[k.strip()] = v.strip()\n        reports[wo['work_order_id']] = {\n            'work_order_id': wo['work_order_id'],\n            'day': int(wo['day']),\n            'machine': report.get('MACHINE', ''),\n            'technician': report.get('TECHNICIAN', ''),\n            'started': report.get('STARTED', ''),\n            'completed': report.get('COMPLETED', ''),\n        }\n    except:\n        pass\n\nvalid_reports = {woid: r for woid, r in reports.items()\n                 if r['machine'] and r['technician']}\n\n# Build maintenance events\nmaintenance_events = []\nfor woid, r in valid_reports.items():\n    day = r['day']\n    completed = time_to_minutes(r['completed'])\n    maintenance_events.append({\n        'day': day,\n        'completed_datetime': day * 1440 + completed,\n        'machine': r['machine'],\n        'technician': r['technician'],\n        'work_order_id': woid,\n    })\n\nmaintenance_events.sort(key=lambda e: (e['machine'], e['completed_datetime']))\nmaint_by_machine = defaultdict(list)\nfor ev in maintenance_events:\n    maint_by_machine[ev['machine']].append(ev)\n\n# ── 3. Process batches with production times ──\n\nbatch_times = {}\nfor a in assignments:\n    day = int(a['day'])\n    job_id = a['job_id']\n    start_t = time_to_minutes(a['start_time'])\n    start_datetime = day * 1440 + start_t\n    batch_times[job_id] = {\n        'start_datetime': start_datetime,\n        'machine_id': a['machine_id'],\n    }\n\nbatch_details = []\nfor b in batches:\n    job_id = b['job_id']\n    if job_id in batch_times:\n        bt = batch_times[job_id]\n        batch_details.append({\n            'batch_id': b['batch_id'],\n            'job_id': job_id,\n            'machine_id': b['machine_id'],\n            'product_id': b['product_id'],\n            'produced_qty': int(b['produced_quantity']),\n            'start_datetime': bt['start_datetime'],\n        })\n\n# ── 4. Link batches to maintenance events ──\n\ndef find_maintenance_for_batch(batch_start, machine_maints):\n    for ev in reversed(machine_maints):\n        if ev['completed_datetime'] <= batch_start:\n            return ev\n    return None\n\ndef find_window_maints(batch_start, machine_maints):\n    \"\"\"Find maint event that applies and its successor\"\"\"\n    prev = None\n    for ev in machine_maints:\n        if ev['completed_datetime'] <= batch_start:\n            prev = ev\n        else:\n            return prev, ev\n    return prev, None\n\nbatch_with_maint = []\nfor b in batch_details:\n    machine_id = b['machine_id']\n    machine_maints = maint_by_machine.get(machine_id, [])\n    if not machine_maints:\n        continue\n    bs = b['start_datetime']\n    prev, next_m = find_window_maints(bs, machine_maints)\n    batch_with_maint.append({\n        **b,\n        'maint_event': prev,\n        'next_maint': next_m,\n    })\n\n# ── 5. Identify rejected batches ──\n\ninspected = {}\nfor insp in inspections:\n    bid = insp['batch_id']\n    inspected[bid] = {\n        'rejected_pct': float(insp['rejected_pct']),\n        'defect_code': insp['defect_code'],\n        'result': insp['inspection_result'],\n        'product_id': insp['product_id'],\n        'inspection_day': int(insp['inspection_day']),\n    }\n\n# Build product thresholds\nprod_thresholds = {r['product_id']: float(r['quality_rejection_threshold_pct'])\n                   for r in products}\n\n# ── 6. Statistical approach to determine which rejections are maintenance-caused ──\n\n# For each product, compute baseline rejection stats (median, std deviation)\n# across all batches (excluding those in maintenance windows)\n\nprod_rej_pcts = defaultdict(list)  # product_id -> list of rejection pcts\nfor b in batch_with_maint:\n    bid = b['batch_id']\n    if bid in inspected:\n        prod_rej_pcts[b['product_id']].append(inspected[bid]['rejected_pct'])\n\n# For each product, compute stats\nimport statistics as stats\n\nprod_stats = {}\nfor pid, pcts in prod_rej_pcts.items():\n    if pcts:\n        med = stats.median(pcts)\n        # Use median absolute deviation\n        mad = stats.median([abs(p - med) for p in pcts])\n        if mad == 0:\n            mad = stats.stdev(pcts) if len(pcts) > 1 else 0.01\n        prod_stats[pid] = {'median': med, 'mad': mad, 'count': len(pcts)}\n\n# ── 7. For each batch in a maintenance window, determine if rejection is \n#     maintenance-caused ──\n\n# A rejected batch is considered \"caused by maintenance\" if:\n# 1. It lies in a maintenance window, AND\n# 2. Its rejection pct is significantly above the product's baseline\n#    (threshold: > median + 2 * MAD or just > threshold)\n# OR\n# 3. Its defect code is unusual (not commonly seen for that product)\n\n# Actually, a simpler approach: \n# A batch is linked to a maintenance event if it's in the window.\n# But we only count a technician as confirmed if there's a PATTERN:\n# - Multiple batches with the same defect code across different work orders\n\n# Let me try: For each (technician, defect_code, product), count linked rejected batches\n# Then confirm the technician if:\n# - For any (technician, defect_code, product) combination, there are ≥2 linked batches\n#   across ≥2 work orders\n\n# Actually, let me try a more direct approach:\n# For each (tech, product), if there are ≥2 rejected batches linked to the tech\n# across ≥2 work orders, that product is affected.\n# And the tech is confirmed if there are ≥2 products affected OR\n# if one product has ≥3 batches across ≥2 work orders.\n\n# Wait, let me re-read the problem:\n# \"A technician is confirmed only if their own maintenance work can be linked to \n# at least 3 distinct rejected batches, distributed across at least 2 different \n# work orders performed by that same technician.\"\n\n# This means: across ALL their work, the technician has ≥3 rejected batches \n# linked to them, spread across ≥2 work orders. The batches can be for any products.\n\n# \"Once a technician is confirmed, include every product for which at least one \n# rejected batch can be causally linked to that technician's faulty maintenance\"\n\n# So after confirming the tech, for each product with ≥1 linked rejected batch,\n# include that product.\n\n# The key question is: WHICH batches are \"causally linked\"? Not all rejected batches\n# in the window. We need to determine which ones are actually caused by the maintenance.\n\n# ── Approach: Use defect code rarity ──\n\n# For each product, compute the frequency distribution of defect codes in REJECTED batches\n# across all time (baseline). Then, for a batch in a maintenance window,\n# if its defect code is rare for that product (appears in < X% of the product's rejections),\n# it's likely maintenance-caused.\n\n# Actually, let me think differently. The maintenance introduces a hidden fault of a \n# specific class. This fault class corresponds to a specific defect code. Different \n# products are susceptible to different defect codes.\n\n# So the approach is:\n# 1. For each maintenance event, look at all rejected batches in the window\n# 2. Find which defect code appears most commonly for this technician across their windows\n# 3. That defect code is likely the \"hidden fault\" they introduce\n# 4. Products with that defect code in the window are affected\n\n# Even simpler: For a given (technician, defect_code) pair, if the same defect_code\n# appears in rejected batches across multiple work orders, it's evidence of a pattern.\n\n# ── 8. Build linkage data ──\n\n# For each rejected batch in a maintenance window, record the link\ntech_links = []  # (tech, wo, prod, defect_code, rej_pct, batch_id)\n\nfor b in batch_with_maint:\n    bid = b['batch_id']\n    if bid not in inspected:\n        continue\n    insp = inspected[bid]\n    if insp['result'] != 'REJECTED':\n        continue\n    ev = b['maint_event']\n    if ev is None:\n        continue\n    tech_links.append({\n        'technician': ev['technician'],\n        'work_order': ev['work_order_id'],\n        'product': b['product_id'],\n        'defect_code': insp['defect_code'],\n        'rejected_pct': insp['rejected_pct'],\n        'batch_id': bid,\n        'machine': b['machine_id'],\n    })\n\n# ── 9. For each technician, find persistent defect code patterns ──\n\n# Group by (technician, defect_code, product)\npattern_counts = defaultdict(set)  # (tech, dc, prod) -> set of (wo, batch_id)\nfor link in tech_links:\n    key = (link['technician'], link['defect_code'], link['product'])\n    pattern_counts[key].add((link['work_order'], link['batch_id']))\n\n# For each technician, find which defect_code/product combinations \n# appear across multiple work orders\ntech_pattern_strength = defaultdict(list)\nfor (tech, dc, prod), entries in pattern_counts.items():\n    unique_wos = set(e[0] for e in entries)\n    tech_pattern_strength[tech].append({\n        'defect_code': dc,\n        'product': prod,\n        'batch_count': len(entries),\n        'wo_count': len(unique_wos),\n    })\n\n# ── 10. Confirm technicians ──\n# A technician is confirmed if across ALL their work:\n# - ≥3 distinct rejected batches \n# - distributed across ≥2 different work orders\n# AND the batches are plausibly caused by a pattern (same defect code persisting)\n\n# First, count ALL rejected batches per technician and work order\ntech_all_batches = defaultdict(set)   # tech -> set of batch_ids\ntech_all_wos = defaultdict(set)       # tech -> set of work_order_ids\nfor link in tech_links:\n    tech_all_batches[link['technician']].add(link['batch_id'])\n    tech_all_wos[link['technician']].add(link['work_order'])\n\n# Count batches with a clear pattern (same defect code across multiple WOs for same product)\ntech_pattern_batches = defaultdict(set)\ntech_pattern_wos = defaultdict(set)\nfor (tech, dc, prod), entries in pattern_counts.items():\n    unique_wos = set(e[0] for e in entries)\n    if len(unique_wos) >= 2 and len(entries) >= 2:\n        tech_pattern_batches[tech].update(e[1] for e in entries)\n        tech_pattern_wos[tech].update(unique_wos)\n    elif len(unique_wos) >= 1 and len(entries) >= 3:\n        # 3+ batches with same (tech, dc, prod) even from 1 WO\n        tech_pattern_batches[tech].update(e[1] for e in entries)\n        tech_pattern_wos[tech].update(unique_wos)\n\nconfirmed_techs = set()\nfor tech in tech_pattern_batches:\n    if len(tech_pattern_batches[tech]) >= 3 and len(tech_pattern_wos[tech]) >= 2:\n        confirmed_techs.add(tech)\n\n# Also check: tech with 3+ batches across 2+ WOs where the batches share a defect_code \n# per product\nfor tech in tech_all_batches:\n    if len(tech_all_batches[tech]) >= 3 and len(tech_all_wos[tech]) >= 2:\n        # Check if there's a pattern: at least one defect_code appears across multiple WOs\n        for entry in tech_pattern_strength[tech]:\n            if entry['batch_count'] >= 2 and entry['wo_count'] >= 2:\n                confirmed_techs.add(tech)\n                break\n        # Also check: 3+ batches with same (tech, dc) regardless of product\n        dc_counter = Counter()\n        wo_per_dc = defaultdict(set)\n        for link in tech_links:\n            if link['technician'] == tech:\n                dc_counter[link['defect_code']] += 1\n                wo_per_dc[link['defect_code']].add(link['work_order'])\n        for dc, cnt in dc_counter.items():\n            if cnt >= 3 and len(wo_per_dc[dc]) >= 2:\n                confirmed_techs.add(tech)\n                break\n        # If no pattern but still has 3+ batches across 2+ WOs, \n        # we should still consider them\n        # Actually, the problem says defects from unrelated factors must be ruled out\n        # So a pattern IS necessary\n\n# ── 11. Determine affected products for each confirmed tech ──\n\ntech_products = defaultdict(set)\nfor tech in confirmed_techs:\n    for link in tech_links:\n        if link['technician'] != tech:\n            continue\n        # Include product if it has at least one linked batch for this tech\n        tech_products[tech].add(link['product'])\n\n# ── 12. Build final answer ──\n\nproduct_techs = defaultdict(set)\nfor tech, prods in tech_products.items():\n    for prod in prods:\n        product_techs[prod].add(tech)\n\nresult = {}\nfor prod in sorted(product_techs.keys()):\n    techs = sorted(product_techs[prod])\n    result[prod] = techs\n\nprint(\"Confirmed technicians:\", sorted(confirmed_techs))\nprint(\"Technician stats:\")\nfor tech in sorted(confirmed_techs):\n    print(f\"  {tech}: {len(tech_all_batches[tech])} batches, {len(tech_all_wos[tech])} WOs, {len(tech_products[tech])} products\")\nprint(f\"\\nTotal products in answer: {len(result)}\")\n\nwith open('/app/answer.json', 'w') as f:\n    json.dump(result, f, indent=2)\nprint(\"Saved to /app/answer.json\")\n","filePath":"/app/solve2.py"},"output":"Wrote file successfully.","metadata":{"diagnostics":{},"filepath":"/app/solve2.py","exists":false,"truncated":false},"title":"app/solve2.py","time":{"start":1781041090290,"end":1781041090297}},"id":"prt_eae51f2eb001iSUgYGg7bxLMPJ","sessionID":"ses_151af1bb2ffeU77q8xSk3g0087","messageID":"msg_eae51be55001bCWPHZs7GzoEAF"}}

{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1781041212008,"sessionID":"ses_151af1bb2ffeU77q8xSk3g0087","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"write","callID":"call_00_cF3VSrqR6WqVU2lBTzjC5107","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"content":"import csv\nimport json\nfrom collections import defaultdict, Counter\n\n# ── 1. Read data ──\ndef read_csv(path):\n    with open(path) as f:\n        return list(csv.DictReader(f))\n\nmachines      = read_csv('/app/machines.csv')\nproducts      = read_csv('/app/products.csv')\ntechnicians   = read_csv('/app/technicians.csv')\nwork_orders   = read_csv('/app/maintenance_work_orders.csv')\nassignments   = read_csv('/app/machine_assignment_log.csv')\nbatches       = read_csv('/app/production_batches.csv')\ninspections   = read_csv('/app/quality_inspections.csv')\nfactory_cond  = read_csv('/app/daily_factory_conditions.csv')\n\nproduct_info = {r['product_id']: r for r in products}\nprod_families = {r['product_id']: r['product_family'] for r in products}\nprod_thresholds = {r['product_id']: float(r['quality_rejection_threshold_pct']) for r in products}\n\ndef time_to_minutes(t):\n    parts = t.strip().split(':')\n    return int(parts[0]) * 60 + int(parts[1])\n\n# ── 2. Parse service reports ──\nreports = {}\nfor wo in work_orders:\n    rpath = '/app/' + wo['report_path']\n    try:\n        with open(rpath) as f:\n            lines = f.readlines()\n        report = {}\n        for line in lines:\n            if ':' in line:\n                k, v = line.strip().split(':', 1)\n                report[k.strip()] = v.strip()\n        reports[wo['work_order_id']] = {\n            'day': int(wo['day']),\n            'machine': report.get('MACHINE', ''),\n            'technician': report.get('TECHNICIAN', ''),\n            'completed': report.get('COMPLETED', ''),\n        }\n    except:\n        pass\n\nvalid_reports = {woid: r for woid, r in reports.items()\n                 if r['machine'] and r['technician']}\n\n# Maintenance events sorted by machine then time\nmaintenance_events = []\nfor woid, r in valid_reports.items():\n    day = r['day']\n    completed = time_to_minutes(r['completed'])\n    maintenance_events.append({\n        'completed_datetime': day * 1440 + completed,\n        'machine': r['machine'],\n        'technician': r['technician'],\n        'work_order_id': woid,\n    })\nmaintenance_events.sort(key=lambda e: (e['machine'], e['completed_datetime']))\n\nmaint_by_machine = defaultdict(list)\nfor ev in maintenance_events:\n    maint_by_machine[ev['machine']].append(ev)\n\n# ── 3. Batch timing ──\nbatch_times = {}\nfor a in assignments:\n    day = int(a['day'])\n    job_id = a['job_id']\n    start_t = time_to_minutes(a['start_time'])\n    start_datetime = day * 1440 + start_t\n    batch_times[job_id] = {\n        'start_datetime': start_datetime,\n        'machine_id': a['machine_id'],\n    }\n\nbatch_details = []\nfor b in batches:\n    job_id = b['job_id']\n    if job_id in batch_times:\n        bt = batch_times[job_id]\n        batch_details.append({\n            'batch_id': b['batch_id'],\n            'job_id': job_id,\n            'machine_id': b['machine_id'],\n            'product_id': b['product_id'],\n            'start_datetime': bt['start_datetime'],\n            'production_day': int(b['production_day']),\n        })\n\n# ── 4. Link batches to maintenance window ──\ndef find_maint_window(batch_start, machine_maints):\n    prev = None\n    for ev in machine_maints:\n        if ev['completed_datetime'] <= batch_start:\n            prev = ev\n        else:\n            return prev, ev\n    return prev, None\n\nbatch_with_maint = []\nfor b in batch_details:\n    machine_id = b['machine_id']\n    machine_maints = maint_by_machine.get(machine_id, [])\n    if not machine_maints:\n        continue\n    prev, next_m = find_maint_window(b['start_datetime'], machine_maints)\n    batch_with_maint.append({\n        **b,\n        'maint_event': prev,\n        'next_maint': next_m,\n    })\n\n# ── 5. Process inspections ──\ninspected = {}\nfor insp in inspections:\n    bid = insp['batch_id']\n    inspected[bid] = {\n        'rejected_pct': float(insp['rejected_pct']),\n        'defect_code': insp['defect_code'],\n        'result': insp['inspection_result'],\n        'product_id': insp['product_id'],\n        'inspection_day': int(insp['inspection_day']),\n    }\n\n# ── 6. Build baseline: for each product, how often is it rejected, \n#       and with which defect codes ──\n\n# For each product, count inspections and rejections by defect code\nprod_insp_count = Counter()\nprod_rej_count = Counter()\nprod_rej_by_dc = defaultdict(Counter)  # product -> defect_code -> count\n\nfor bid, info in inspected.items():\n    pid = info['product_id']\n    prod_insp_count[pid] += 1\n    if info['result'] == 'REJECTED':\n        prod_rej_count[pid] += 1\n        prod_rej_by_dc[pid][info['defect_code']] += 1\n\n# ── 7. Determine signature defect codes per technician ──\n\n# For each tech, collect defect codes from linked rejected batches across\n# all their work orders\ntech_dc_counter = defaultdict(Counter)  # tech -> defect_code -> count\ntech_wo_dc = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(set))  # tech -> dc -> set of work_order_ids\n\n# Also collect per-product\ntech_prod_dc = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(Counter))  # tech -> product -> dc -> count\ntech_prod_wo = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(set)))  # tech -> prod -> dc -> set of WOs\n\nfor b in batch_with_maint:\n    bid = b['batch_id']\n    if bid not in inspected:\n        continue\n    info = inspected[bid]\n    if info['result'] != 'REJECTED':\n        continue\n    ev = b['maint_event']\n    if ev is None:\n        continue\n    tech = ev['technician']\n    dc = info['defect_code']\n    prod = b['product_id']\n    wo = ev['work_order_id']\n    \n    tech_dc_counter[tech][dc] += 1\n    tech_wo_dc[tech][dc].add(wo)\n    tech_prod_dc[tech][prod][dc] += 1\n    tech_prod_wo[tech][prod][dc].add(wo)\n\n# For each technician, find the defect codes that are \"signature\"\n# A signature defect code is one that appears in ≥2 different WOs\n# (indicating a pattern, not a one-off)\n\ntech_signature_dcs = defaultdict(set)\nfor tech, dc_counter in tech_dc_counter.items():\n    for dc, wo_set in tech_wo_dc[tech].items():\n        if len(wo_set) >= 2:\n            tech_signature_dcs[tech].add(dc)\n    # Also, if a defect code has ≥3 rejections even from 1 WO, consider it\n    for dc, count in dc_counter.items():\n        if count >= 5:\n            tech_signature_dcs[tech].add(dc)\n\n# ── 8. Now re-link: only count rejections whose defect code matches \n#       the technician's signature ──\n\n# For each tech, collect batches with signature defect codes\ntech_batches = defaultdict(set)     # tech -> set of batch_ids\ntech_wos = defaultdict(set)         # tech -> set of work_order_ids\ntech_prod_batches = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(set))  # tech -> product -> set of batch_ids\n\nfor b in batch_with_maint:\n    bid = b['batch_id']\n    if bid not in inspected:\n        continue\n    info = inspected[bid]\n    if info['result'] != 'REJECTED':\n        continue\n    ev = b['maint_event']\n    if ev is None:\n        continue\n    tech = ev['technician']\n    dc = info['defect_code']\n    \n    # Only count if the defect code is a signature for this technician\n    if dc in tech_signature_dcs[tech]:\n        tech_batches[tech].add(bid)\n        tech_wos[tech].add(ev['work_order_id'])\n        tech_prod_batches[tech][b['product_id']].add(bid)\n\n# ── 9. Confirm technicians ──\nconfirmed = set()\nfor tech in tech_batches:\n    if len(tech_batches[tech]) >= 3 and len(tech_wos[tech]) >= 2:\n        confirmed.add(tech)\n\n# ── 10. For confirmed techs, include each product that has ≥1 linked batch ──\ntech_products = defaultdict(set)\nfor tech in confirmed:\n    for prod in tech_prod_batches[tech]:\n        tech_products[tech].add(prod)\n\n# ── 11. Build answer ──\nproduct_techs = defaultdict(set)\nfor tech, prods in tech_products.items():\n    for prod in prods:\n        product_techs[prod].add(tech)\n\nresult = {}\nfor prod in sorted(product_techs.keys()):\n    result[prod] = sorted(product_techs[prod])\n\nprint(\"Signature defect codes per technician:\")\nfor tech in sorted(tech_signature_dcs):\n    print(f\"  {tech}: {sorted(tech_signature_dcs[tech])}\")\n\nprint(f\"\\nConfirmed technicians ({len(confirmed)}):\")\nfor tech in sorted(confirmed):\n    print(f\"  {tech}: {len(tech_batches[tech])} batches, {len(tech_wos[tech])} WOs, {len(tech_products[tech])} products\")\n    print(f\"    Products: {sorted(tech_products[tech])}\")\n\nwith open('/app/answer.json', 'w') as f:\n    json.dump(result, f, indent=2)\nprint(f\"\\nAnswer saved. Hidden faults are never labeled as such in the data.\n- A hidden fault affects later batches produced on the same machine until a subsequent maintenance event changes the machine state.\n- A given fault class may affect only some products. A technician can therefore be responsible for quality issues on one product while leaving other products on the same machine unaffected.\n- Quality inspection happens with a delay, so inspection date is not the same as production date.\n- Defects also arise from unrelated factors and natural variation that must be ruled out.\"\n\nOK so the key mechanics are:\n1. Machine has a persistent technical condition (not recorded)\n2. Maintenance can change this condition (for better, worse, or no change)\n3. A hidden fault (if introduced) persists until next maintenance\n4. The fault has a \"fault class\" that affects only certain products\n5. Other defects (natural variation) happen too\n\nThe hidden fault is a change in the machine's condition that affects quality. The \"fault class\" is the type of quality issue it causes (defect code). Different products may be immune to certain fault classes (e.g., product A might fail if the machine has alignment issues, but product B might be fine).\n\nSo to determine if a rejection is caused by maintenance:\n1. The batch must fall in the window after the maintenance\n2. The product must be susceptible to the fault class introduced\n3. The rejection must not be explainable by other factors\n\nThe challenge is we don't know what fault class was introduced by each maintenance.\n\nOne approach that avoids needing to know the fault class explicitly:\n- For each maintenance event, look at the products rejected in its window\n- If multiple batches of the same product type are rejected after the maintenance (but not before), that's strong evidence the maintenance affected that product\n- And if other products on the same machine are unaffected (not rejected, or rejected with different defect codes), that confirms the fault class is product-specific\n\nLet me try yet another approach: Track the rejection rate per (machine, product) over time and look for changes after maintenance events.\n\nActually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. in ≥2 of the technician's work orders\ntech_signature = defaultdict(set)\ntech_dc_wo = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(set))\nfor tech, links in tech_link_batches.items():\n    for link in links:\n        tech_dc_wo[tech][link['defect_code']].add(link['work_order'])\n    for dc, wos in tech_dc_wo[tech].items():\n        if len(wos) >= 2:\n            tech_signature[tech].add(dc)\n\n# Now filter: This is purely about the temporal relationship. The hidden fault is a specific fault class that manifests as a specific defect code. Products that get rejected with that defect code after the maintenance are affected. Products that get rejected with OTHER defect codes are experiencing non-maintenance issues.\n\nBut we don't know which defect code the hidden fault corresponds to! That's the challenge.
